# **Advanced Architectures in Latent Neural Video Coding: A Technical Evaluation of LeWM-VC and the Joint Embedding Predictive Paradigm**

## **The Paradigm Shift in Video Compression: From Pixel Reconstruction to Semantic Prediction**

The global landscape of digital communication is currently defined by an unprecedented surge in video data, which accounted for approximately 82% of all internet traffic by the end of 2025\.1 This exponential growth has placed immense pressure on existing video coding standards, such as High Efficiency Video Coding (HEVC/H.265) and Versatile Video Coding (VVC/H.266), which rely on classical signal processing techniques including block-based motion compensation, discrete cosine transforms (DCT), and arithmetic entropy coding.1 While these standards have successfully served the industry for decades, they are increasingly hindered by the "perception-distortion" bound, where achieving higher compression ratios inevitably leads to severe visual degradation, such as blocking artifacts and blurring, particularly in ultra-low bitrate scenarios below 0.03 bits per pixel (bpp).3

In response to these limitations, a fundamental architectural shift is occurring: the transition from generative, reconstruction-based codecs to predictive, latent-space architectures.6 At the forefront of this movement is LeWM-VC (Learning Energy-based Model for Video Coding), an implementation of Yann LeCun’s Joint Embedding Predictive Architecture (JEPA).6 Unlike traditional neural video codecs (NVCs) that utilize autoencoders to reconstruct every pixel of a frame, LeWM-VC operates as a "mental simulator" that predicts the mathematical embeddings of future or missing frames within a compact, latent semantic space.10 This approach recognizes that for many modern applications—ranging from autonomous vehicle perception to robotic planning and semantic search—the exact pixel-level fidelity of noisy backgrounds is far less critical than the structural and temporal consistency of the objects within the scene.9

The strategic positioning of LeWM-VC is rooted in its ability to avoid "representation collapse"—a common failure mode in self-supervised learning where a model learns trivial, non-informative features.14 By integrating Sketched Isotropic Gaussian Regularization (SIGReg), LeWM-VC provides a mathematically rigorous framework for stabilizing end-to-end training without the complex heuristics, such as stop-gradients or momentum encoders, that have made previous joint-embedding models difficult to scale.6 This transition toward energy-based, latent world models represents not merely an incremental improvement in coding efficiency, but a total reimagining of what a video codec should be: a system that understands the world it is compressing.10

## **Technical Foundations of LeWM-VC: The JEPA Framework and Energy-Based Modeling**

LeWM-VC is built upon the Joint Embedding Predictive Architecture (JEPA), a self-supervised learning framework designed to capture the underlying structure of data without the computational overhead of pixel-level reconstruction.6 The architecture is composed of two primary neural components: an encoder and a predictor.6 The encoder, ![][image1], maps a raw video observation ![][image2] into a low-dimensional latent representation ![][image3]. In the LeWM implementation, this representation is exceptionally compact, often consisting of a single 192-dimensional token per frame, which is approximately 200 times fewer tokens than those used by foundation-model-based world models such as DINO-WM.6

The predictor, ![][image4], models the dynamics of the environment within this latent space. Given a context of past latent embeddings ![][image5] and an optional action or temporal prompt ![][image6], the predictor generates a forecast of the next embedding ![][image7].6 This predictive process is governed by the principles of Energy-Based Models (EBMs), which define a scalar energy function ![][image8] that measures the compatibility between an input ![][image9] and a predicted output ![][image10].7 In the context of video coding, low energy indicates high compatibility between the predicted latent state and the actual observed latent state, while high energy signals "surprise" or a deviation from expected world dynamics.7

The training objective for LeWM-VC is remarkably simple, consisting of only two loss terms:

![][image11]  
Where ![][image12] is the mean squared error (MSE) between the predicted embedding ![][image13] and the ground-truth embedding ![][image14] produced by a target encoder, and ![][image15] is the regularization term that ensures the latent space remains well-distributed.6 This reduction in tunable hyperparameters—from as many as six in previous end-to-end world models to just one in LeWM—greatly enhances the accessibility and reproducibility of the research.6

### **Comparative Architectural Analysis of World Models**

| Feature | LeWorldModel (LeWM) | DINO-WM | VideoMAE |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| Model Size | \~15 Million Parameters | \>1 Billion Parameters | Variable (Encoder-heavy) |
| Representation Space | Latent Embedding | Patch-feature Tensors | Pixel Space |
| Training Objective | Prediction \+ SIGReg | Frozen DINOv2 \+ Prediction | Masked Reconstruction |
| Hardware Required | Single GPU (few hours) | Multi-node Cluster | High-end GPU Clusters |
| Planning Speed | \~1 Second | \~47 Seconds | N/A (Generative only) |
| Token Density | 1 token / frame | \~200 tokens / frame | N/A (Pixels) |
| Source | 6 | 6 | 8 |

The extreme efficiency of LeWM, as shown in the table above, allows for planning and inference speeds that are up to 48 times faster than those of foundation-model-based alternatives.6 This makes LeWM-VC uniquely suited for real-time video coding and robotic control tasks where low latency is a critical requirement.7

## **JEPA-Based Temporal Prediction and the Absence of Motion Vectors**

A significant innovation within the LeWM-VC framework is its approach to temporal prediction without the use of explicit motion vectors.9 Traditional video coding standards rely on motion estimation and compensation (ME/MC), where the encoder searches for the best matching block in a previous reference frame and transmits a displacement vector (motion vector) and a residual error.1 While effective, this process is computationally expensive and struggles with complex deformations, occlusions, and non-linear lighting changes.2

JEPA-based models like V-JEPA (Video Joint Embedding Predictive Architecture) and LeWM replace this algorithmic module with a spatiotemporal transformer backbone that processes video as a 3D grid of patches.9 By using a Dense Predictive Loss—a masking-based objective where all tokens contribute to the training loss—the model is forced to learn explicit spatial and temporal grounding.19 When parts of the video are masked, the predictor must utilize its learned understanding of "persistence, occlusion, and dynamics" to infer the latent representation of the missing content.9

This method of temporal prediction offers several advantages for next-generation video coding:

1. **Semantic Consistency**: The model learns to map similar objects to the same principal components within the latent space, ensuring that an object’s identity is preserved even as its visual appearance changes due to motion or perspective.19  
2. **Disentanglement of Motion and Content**: Early research into MC-JEPA (Motion-Content JEPA) demonstrated that a shared encoder can successfully separate "how things change" from "what things are," allowing for more efficient bitrate allocation.9  
3. **Causal Inductive Bias**: Advanced iterations like C-JEPA introduce object-centric masking, where the entire trajectory of an object is hidden.12 This forces the model to perform "interaction reasoning"—inferring an object's behavior based on how it affects other objects in the scene—thereby preventing the "lazy momentum" shortcut where the model simply assumes objects move in straight lines.12  
4. **Compute Efficiency**: By operating in a compressed latent space rather than a high-dimensional pixel space, the predictor does not require a heavy image decoder to translate its predictions back into raw pixels during the temporal modeling phase, drastically reducing memory overhead.12

Research has shown that this latent-space prediction significantly outperforms pixel-space objectives for understanding motion dynamics, as measured by performance on benchmarks such as Something-Something v2 and Epic-Kitchens-100.19 For instance, V-JEPA achieved state-of-the-art results in action anticipation, surpassing previous task-specific models by leveraging its dense representation of temporal consistency.19

## **SIGReg Regularization: Preventing Representation Collapse in Latent Spaces**

One of the primary hurdles in developing joint-embedding architectures for video coding is the tendency for these models to suffer from representational collapse.14 Collapse occurs when the encoder projects all inputs into a single point or a very low-dimensional subspace, satisfying the predictive objective but discarding all useful information.9 To prevent this, earlier models like I-JEPA relied on brittle engineering hacks, such as stop-gradients, momentum-updated teacher networks, and specialized normalization layers.6

LeWM-VC addresses this through the introduction of Sketched Isotropic Gaussian Regularization (SIGReg).14 SIGReg is a mathematically rigorous regularization method that gently pushes the distribution of learned embeddings toward an isotropic Gaussian distribution, ![][image16].15 This target distribution is chosen because it is the maximum-entropy distribution for a fixed energy level, effectively ensuring that feature vectors are maximally spread throughout the available latent space.16

### **The Mechanics of SIGReg and the Cramér-Wold Theorem**

Directly matching high-dimensional distributions is computationally intractable due to the "curse of dimensionality".16 SIGReg sidesteps this by utilizing the Cramér-Wold theorem, which states that two random vectors share the same distribution if and only if all of their one-dimensional projected marginals are identical.15

The SIGReg algorithm operates through a simple, four-step process:

1. **Random Projection**: The high-dimensional latent embeddings are projected onto multiple randomly sampled one-dimensional directions.15  
2. **Marginal Estimation**: For each direction, the model estimates the empirical distribution of the projected points within a minibatch.15  
3. **Goodness-of-Fit Testing**: Each 1D projection is compared to a 1D Gaussian distribution using the Epps-Pulley test.16 This test minimizes the discrepancy between the characteristic function of the projected features and the closed-form characteristic function of the target Gaussian.16  
4. **Averaging and Optimization**: The mismatches across all directions are averaged to form the SIGReg loss, which is then added to the overall training objective.15

This approach is highly scalable, with computational complexity that grows linearly with both the data size and the latent dimensionality.14 It has been shown to stabilize training even in 512-D or 1024-D settings using very few projection directions.15 Furthermore, SIGReg provides a modality-agnostic anchor, allowing for the joint learning of unified representations from heterogeneous sensors, such as camera-aligned LiDAR or thermal data, without requiring manual alignment labels.21

### **SIGReg Hyperparameter Dynamics**

| Parameter State | Effect on Latent Space | Practical Outcome | Source |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **![][image17]** Too High | Dominant Regularization | Embeddings become perfectly uniform; loss of discriminative power. | 6 |
| ![][image17] Too Low | Weak Regularization | Predictive loss causes collapse; all embeddings aggregate in a low-rank subspace. | 6 |
| ![][image17] Balanced | Optimal Dispersion | Embeddings are spread enough to avoid collapse but structured enough to encode semantic distinctions. | 11 |

In robotic manipulation experiments, such as picking up a red cube, LeWM models trained with SIGReg achieved a "softrank" of approximately 75\.22 While this indicates that the model successfully avoided total collapse, researchers identified a "discriminability gap" where the model still struggled to differentiate between very similar goal states, suggesting that further refinement of the ![][image17] hyperparameter or the inclusion of multi-view data may be necessary for high-precision tasks.22

## **Semantic Surprise Detection and Adaptive Information Gating**

A unique advantage of the energy-based formulation in LeWM-VC is the intrinsic ability to perform semantic surprise detection.6 In this context, "surprise" is defined as the standardized prediction error between the predicted latent state ![][image7] and the actual observed latent state ![][image18].6 High energy levels in the energy-based model indicate that the incoming video data does not conform to the model's internal understanding of world dynamics.7

This mechanism has profound implications for the future of adaptive video streaming and surveillance.17 Rather than transmitting a continuous, high-bitrate stream of largely redundant frames, a surprise-gated policy can be implemented. In such a system, the codec only allocates high bitrate or triggers an "action" (such as a keyframe update or a residual transmission) when the calculated surprise exceeds an adaptive quantile threshold.17

### **Case Study: Surprise-Gated Efficiency in Urban Monitoring**

A study involving a predictive-coding-inspired controller for urban climate monitoring demonstrated the power of surprise-gated policies.17 The system was tasked with monitoring parameters such as heat index, solar irradiance, and wind speed. Compared to a traditional reactive threshold baseline that required 20,930 hours of actuation, the surprise-gated policy required only 962 hours—a 95.4% reduction in energy and bandwidth consumption while maintaining high precision (0.97) and reliable detection of sudden environmental departures.17

Applying this principle to video coding, a LeWM-VC stream could maintain an ultra-low-bitrate latent background for predictable portions of a video (e.g., a static street view) and only burst to high-resolution detail when a "surprising" event occurs, such as a vehicle collision or a person falling.6 This shift from pixel-based redundancy to semantic-based surprise allows for a more efficient utilization of bandwidth that aligns with the "interestingness" of the content to the final observer, whether that observer is a human or an AI analytical system.24

Furthermore, surprise detection serves as a powerful tool for anomaly recognition in security applications.23 By learning "normal" movement patterns through trajectories and spatiotemporal embeddings, JEPA-based models can identify abnormal behaviors, such as emotional shifts in mental health monitoring or unusual pedestrian trajectories in urban backstreets, with accuracies as high as 97.8%.23

## **The Industry Landscape: Strategic Acquisitions and AI-Native Standardization**

The transition toward AI-based video coding is not merely a theoretical research endeavor but a rapidly accelerating industrial reality. This is evidenced by the strategic acquisition of Deep Render by InterDigital, Inc. on October 30, 2025\.27 InterDigital, a leader in mobile and video research with significant intellectual property (IP) in established standards like VVC and HEVC, acquired the London-based AI startup to solidify its leadership in the next generation of AI-native video technologies.28

### **The Significance of the Deep Render Acquisition**

Deep Render, founded in 2018, was a pioneer in developing end-to-end AI-driven video compression technology that sought to replace the entire traditional codec pipeline with a neural network.28 This acquisition represents a pivotal moment in the industry for several reasons:

1. **IP Portfolio Expansion**: The deal integrates Deep Render's entire AI-based video coding patent portfolio into InterDigital's already market-leading video assets.29 In the video coding market, where licensing and standard-essential patents (SEPs) are core revenue drivers, this move secures InterDigital's position for future licensing cycles.30  
2. **Talent Acquisition**: A world-class team of AI experts from Deep Render joined InterDigital's Video Lab, accelerating their internal R\&D capabilities for AI-native video research.28  
3. **Shift Toward NPUs**: Traditional codecs rely heavily on specialized hardware decoders. However, the success of neural network methods like those from Deep Render allows codecs to run on general-purpose hardware like NPUs (Neural Processing Units), reducing the time-to-market for new standards and increasing the flexibility of deployment.2

This acquisition is part of a broader trend where major technology companies are pivoting toward "tensor codecs"—repurposing existing video-codec hardware on GPUs to compress AI model data, thereby supporting large-scale model deployment at lower costs.32

### **Comparative Market Positioning of Video Coding Entities**

| Entity | Role | Strategic Focus | Recent Action | Source |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| InterDigital | Standards & IP Leader | AI-native video research | Acquired Deep Render (Oct 2025\) | 27 |
| Deep Render | AI Specialist | End-to-end AI compression | Acquired by InterDigital | 27 |
| Samsung Research | Global R\&D | Hybrid AI-Traditional Codecs | Developing AI-based In-Loop Filters | 2 |
| NVIDIA | Hardware & Software | Tensor Compression | Repurposing codec hardware for LLM data | 32 |
| AJA / Comprimato | Infrastructure | Video Encoding Software | M\&A to enhance streaming infrastructure | 30 |

The merger of traditional research giants like InterDigital with cutting-edge startups like Deep Render suggests that the industry is moving away from a "component-replacement" strategy—where AI merely augments a traditional codec—toward an "AI-native" paradigm where the entire system is designed around neural representations like those in LeWM-VC.2

## **Evaluation Metrics and Pitfalls in Neural Video Coding Research**

As the field of neural video coding (NVC) matures, the methodology for evaluating performance has come under intense scrutiny, particularly regarding the use of Bjøntegaard Delta (BD-rate) and bits-per-pixel (BPP) calculations.33 In traditional video compression research, performance is evaluated by computing the rate-distortion (RD) curve and the BD-rate on a per-video basis, then averaging the resulting BD-rates across a dataset.33

However, a common pitfall in the learned video compression community is the practice of averaging the RD curves themselves over all videos in a dataset before calculating a single BD-rate.33 This seemingly straightforward approach can lead to significant inaccuracies:

* **Outlier Skewing**: A single video sequence with unique characteristics can disproportionately influence the average RD curve, potentially masking a codec's superior performance on the majority of the dataset.33  
* **Operating Range Mismatch**: When codecs operate at different bitrate ranges, averaging the curves produces inconsistent results, especially if the curves are not linear.33

### **The Perception-Distortion Trade-off and Metric Gaming**

Another challenge is the reliance on traditional metrics like Peak Signal-to-Noise Ratio (PSNR) and Multi-Scale Structural Similarity (MS-SSIM). While standard for measuring reconstruction error, these metrics do not always correlate with human perceptual quality.3 This has led to the adoption of VMAF (Video Multi-method Assessment Fusion), which has a higher correlation with human perception.35

However, VMAF itself is susceptible to "gaming." For example, applying an "unsharp" filter can significantly improve a VMAF score (as much as \-21.67% BD-VMAF) while actually making the video more expensive to encode and resulting in a massive degradation in pixel-fidelity (as seen in a \+139% BD-PSNR increase).36 To combat this, researchers are increasingly turning to VMAF-NEG (No Enhancement Gain), which is designed to be more robust against such tuning attacks by discounting high-frequency content that mimics sharpness without adding information.35

### **Metric Performance Comparison on Reference Sequences**

| Sequence | Metric | Result (vs. x264) | Interpretability | Source |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| videoSRC29 | BD-VMAF | \-55.35% | Headline win (High bitrate saturation) | 36 |
| videoSRC29 | BD-VMAF-NEG | \-12.29% | Realistic gain (One-quarter magnitude) | 36 |
| Beauty | BD-PSNR | \+535.08% | Severe failure (Artifacts/Noise from "unsharp") | 36 |
| Average (Kelvin) | BD-VMAF-NEG | \-5.21% | Perceptually honest gain across 6/7 seqs | 36 |

This data underscores the necessity of multi-metric evaluation to ensure that "quality-honest" gains are being made rather than just exploiting the mathematical weaknesses of a single objective.36 For LeWM-VC, which focuses on semantic features, the use of latent probing—training a lightweight supervised probe to predict physical quantities from the latent embedding—has become a more relevant way to measure whether the codec is capturing the core structure of the world.6

## **Mechanistic Interpretability: Opening the "Black Box" of LeWM Latents**

To ensure that LeWM-VC is truly capturing semantic and physical structures, researchers have turned to mechanistic interpretability.13 This involves probing the latent space to identify specific features that correspond to human-understandable concepts or physical variables.13

Through a process of activation harvesting from the world model's encoder and predictor, followed by decomposition using a Sparse Autoencoder (SAE), specific semantic features have been identified within the LeWM latent space.22 These features "fire" or activate at high values during distinct phases of interaction tasks:

1. **Feature 90 (Tactile Engagement)**: Activates during the moment of coupling or contact between objects.22  
2. **Feature 743 (Alignment Precision)**: Fires during the handover or precise alignment phase prior to a lifting motion.22  
3. **Feature 358 (Spatial Lockdown)**: Activates during the stabilization phase after an object has been successfully manipulated.22

These findings suggest that modern video world models do not necessarily use factorized representations of physical variables (like a classical physics engine) but instead utilize high-dimensional population structures with circular or complex geometries to represent concepts like direction and engagement.13 By understanding these "semantic neurons," future video codecs could implement "latent steering"—using identified features as reward boosters or priority markers during the encoding process to ensure that the most semantically relevant parts of the video are preserved with the highest fidelity.22

## **Future Directions: Hierarchical World Models and Ultra-Low Bitrate Deployment**

As the development of LeWM-VC and similar JEPA-based architectures continues, several key areas for future research have been identified:

* **Hierarchical World Models**: Current JEPA models predict at a single temporal resolution. However, intelligent planning and video understanding often require reasoning at multiple timescales.37 Hierarchical models could learn to predict fine-grained dynamics for local motion and coarse-grained abstractions for long-horizon scene understanding.37  
* **Domain Shift Robustness**: Neural video codecs often struggle when moving between different content styles, such as transitioning from natural scenes to animated sequences.38 Instance-adaptive fine-tuning has been proposed as a solution, offering BD-rate savings of 17% to 27% over base models by tailoring the codec to a specific video instance.38  
* **Diffusion-Based Refinement**: For ultra-low bitrates where latent prediction might still produce visual ambiguity, integrated diffusion models (e.g., DiffVC or S2VC) are being explored.4 These models use temporal context from previously decoded frames to guide a diffusion process, generating high-quality results from very few bits by "filling in" missing details with realistic textures.4  
* **Legal and Ethical Compliance**: As AI codecs become standard, the "AI footprint" and data rights of the training datasets will undergo rigorous technical due diligence.42 Compliance with emerging frameworks like the EU AI Act—particularly Article 50 regarding the marking and labeling of AI-generated or AI-compressed content—will be essential for commercial success.42

## **Conclusion: The Integrated Future of AI and Video Coding**

The research into LeWM-VC and the broader joint-embedding landscape demonstrates that the future of video coding lies in the convergence of semantic understanding and efficient compression. By leveraging JEPA-based temporal prediction and the stabilizing influence of SIGReg regularization, LeWM-VC provides a blueprint for codecs that are not just data-efficient but world-aware. The ability to detect semantic surprise and prioritize "interesting" information offers a path toward massive bitrate reductions that were previously unthinkable under the traditional pixel-reconstruction paradigm.

The strategic moves by industry leaders like InterDigital to acquire pioneers like Deep Render signal that the commercial transition to AI-native video is already underway. While significant hurdles remain in evaluating these systems accurately and deploying them on commodity hardware, the rapid progress in Implicit Neural Representations and NPU-optimized architectures suggests that the "mental simulator" of LeWM-VC will soon be a standard component of our global communication infrastructure. This evolution marks the end of the era of "dumb" pixel-pushing and the beginning of an era of intelligent, latent-space video delivery.
